articles.title: Stay Safe: CHP Urges Precautions Against Heat-Related Illnesses During Extreme Weather - 巴士的報
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
articles.full_text:
DH reminds public to take precautions against heat-related illnesses during very hot weather
The Centre for Health Protection (CHP) of the Department of Health (DH) today (July 24) reminded members of the public to take appropriate precautions against heat-related illnesses, such as heat cramps, heat exhaustion and heat stroke, during very hot weather.
In a high-temperature environment, excessive sweating and difficulty in heat dissipation can cause stress to the body. Members of the public should take the following precautions:
- Hydrate in a timely manner to prevent dehydration;
- Wear light-coloured, loose-fitting and breathable clothing to minimise heat absorption and facilitate perspiration and heat dissipation;
- Keep indoor areas well ventilated and maintain appropriate temperature and humidity, e.g. by opening windows or using a fan or air-conditioning;
- Avoid strenuous exercise and prolonged activities such as hiking or trekking;
- Arrange outdoor activities in the morning or the late afternoon, if possible, and bring enough water; and
- Avoid beverages containing caffeine, such as coffee and tea, as well as alcoholic beverages, which may speed up water loss through the urinary system.
In addition, infants and children, the elderly, pregnant women, individuals with chronic illnesses such as heart disease or high blood pressure, overweight people and outdoor/manual workers are more susceptible to heat stroke. These groups should pay special attention to the following:
- Infants and young children: Avoid going outside when the temperature is high. If they must go outside, adults should ensure they stay in places with moderate temperatures and good ventilation or air-conditioning. Infants and young children should never be left unattended in a confined space such as a vehicle;
- The elderly, pregnant women, individuals with chronic illnesses and overweight people: Pay close attention to health conditions and seek help as soon as possible if experiencing any abnormalities in health indicators such as blood pressure, heart rate, etc or if there are fluctuations in health conditions. Avoid going out when the temperature is high. Make sure there is good ventilation, or turn on a fan or air-conditioner to keep an appropriate temperature indoors. High temperatures can also worsen the condition of people with chronic illnesses (such as cardiovascular and respiratory diseases). Therefore, they should have an ample supply of their usual medication and keep in contact with family members, neighbours and the community. People should seek immediate help if they feel unwell.
- Outdoor/manual workers: Reschedule work to cooler hours as far as feasible. If working in a hot environment is unavoidable, provide shade where practicable and use ventilation and heat dissipation equipment. Once work has begun, gradually adjust the pace of work and take breaks in the shade to regain strength.
The DH reminded the public that heat stroke is one of the most common health risks in hot weather. Over the past five years, there have been over 200 hospital admissions due to heat stroke each year in Hong Kong, with two to five fatal cases occurring mainly during the summer months from May to September. Vulnerable groups are more susceptible to heat stroke. Symptoms include dizziness, headache, nausea, shortness of breath or confusion. In severe cases, patients may suffer from convulsions or become unconscious, which can be life-threatening if they are not cooled down and resuscitated in a timely manner.
Members of the public are also advised to pay attention to the latest weather, as well as the real-time and forecasted ultraviolet (UV) index released by the Hong Kong Observatory (HKO). When the UV index is high (6 or above), the public should note the following:
- Avoid direct sunlight exposure to the skin and the eyes;
- Apply broad-spectrum, water-resistant sunscreen liberally, with a sun protection factor (SPF) of at least 30 or higher, when participating in outdoor activities. Reapply every two hours if staying outdoors for long periods, or after swimming, sweating or towelling off;
- While using DEET-containing insect repellents for personal protection against mosquito-borne diseases, apply sunscreen before insect repellent;
If symptoms develop, such as dizziness, headache, nausea, shortness of breath or confusion, rest and seek help immediately, and seek medical advice as soon as possible.
For more health information, the public may visit the CHP's heat stroke page and UV radiation page; the HKO's Dial-a-Weather (1878 200), latest weather report and forecast, UV Index and weather information for hiking and mountaineering; and press releases of the Labour Department on precautions against heat stroke for outdoor workers and their employers when the Heat Stress at Work Warning is in force.
Hot weather in Hong Kong
CFS announces Second Hong Kong Total Diet Study findings on cadmium and lead
The Centre for Food Safety (CFS) of the Food and Environmental Hygiene Department today (July 24) released the fifth report under the Second Hong Kong Total Diet Study (2nd HKTDS), which assessed the levels of cadmium and lead in food and the overall dietary exposure to these metallic contaminants among the Hong Kong population.
Cadmium and lead are metallic elements that are present ubiquitously in the environment. They can be released through various natural processes or human activities, and then enter the human food chain. The study findings showed that the dietary exposure estimates of the local adult and younger populations to cadmium were below the provisional tolerable monthly intake established by the Joint Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations/World Health Organization Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA), while those of lead fell below the respective dietary exposure levels identified by JECFA, indicating that the health impacts on the local population are also low. The dietary exposure estimates of the overall adult population to cadmium and lead were lower than those reported in the First Hong Kong Total Diet Study conducted between 2010 and 2014.
Out of a total of 187 food items covering 15 food groups tested in the Study, 125 (67 per cent) were detected with cadmium, while lead was detected in 176 (94 per cent). The three major food groups, namely "cereals and their products", "vegetables and their products" and "fish, seafood and their products", accounted for about 80 per cent of the total dietary exposure to cadmium and about 50 per cent of the total dietary exposure to lead in the local population.
According to the safety evaluation conducted by JECFA, long-term excessive exposure to cadmium may affect kidney functions, while long-term exposure to lead may cause damage to the nervous system and hypertension. Nevertheless, the above study findings showed that the dietary exposure estimates of the local population to cadmium and lead all fell below the respective levels identified by JECFA, thus the health impacts on the local population are low. A spokesman for the CFS reminded members of the public to maintain a balanced and varied diet. Moreover, additional food preparation steps, including washing vegetables and fruits thoroughly to remove dust and soil, removing outer leaves from leafy greens and peeling off the skin of root crops, may be adopted to reduce cadmium and lead levels therein.
The 2nd HKTDS aims to estimate the latest dietary exposure of the Hong Kong population and various population subgroups to a range of chemical substances of potential food safety concern, and to assess the associated health risks. The public can download the study reports released thus far from the CFS webpage at www.cfs.gov.hk/english/programme/programme_firm/programme_tds_2nd_HKTDS_report.html.
Source: AI-found images

articles.title: Labour Department Issues Heat Stress Warning: Employers Urged to Protect Workers from Heat Stroke - 巴士的報
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
articles.full_text:
LD reminds employers and employees to take heat stroke preventive measures in times of Heat Stress at Work Warning
As the Heat Stress at Work Warning is now in effect, the Labour Department (LD) reminds employers and employees to take appropriate measures during the effective period of the warning to prevent heat stroke when working in hot weather or hot environments.
The Labour Department, Photo source: reference image
Employees who work outdoors or in non-air-conditioned indoor environments face high levels of heat stress and are at a relatively higher risk of heat stroke. Employers should assess the risk factors of heat stress for employees at work and, based on the identified risk factors, take necessary preventive and control measures, including rescheduling work periods, setting up shading covers, providing ventilation and heat dissipation equipment, and reminding employees to replenish water and rest in a timely manner.
The Heat Stress at Work Warning is formulated by the LD based on the Hong Kong Heat Index. There are three levels of the warning: Amber, Red and Black, which help employers and employees better understand the level of heat stress while working outdoors or indoors without air-conditioning systems.
A spokesman for the LD said that when the department issues the Heat Stress at Work Warning, employers must refer to the criteria and recommendations provided in the "Guidance Notes on Prevention of Heat Stroke at Work" to conduct risk assessments, according to the workloads and other relevant heat stress risk factors, for employees who work outdoors or in non-air-conditioned indoor workplaces. Appropriate rest breaks should be arranged every hour, as far as reasonably practicable, based on various levels of the Heat Stress at Work Warning, to reduce employees' risk of heat stroke.
Employees must also follow instructions to rest on time. Whenever there are any symptoms of heat-related illnesses, such as headache, dizziness, thirst, and nausea, they should rest in a cool and shady place, drink water, and inform employers/supervisors to take appropriate action immediately.
The LD issued the "Guidance Notes on Prevention of Heat Stroke at Work", detailing the various risk factors that should be considered when conducting heat stress risk assessments and recommending corresponding control measures for identified risk factors for employers' and employees' reference. For the Heat Stress at Work Warning and related guidelines, please refer to the department's thematic webpage: www.labour.gov.hk/eng/news/prevention_of_heat_stroke_at_work.htm.
Heat Stress at Work Warning
Hong Kong Customs detects suspected case of illegal importing of live plant pests
Hong Kong Customs yesterday (July 23) detected a suspected case of illegal importing of live plant pests at the Lo Wu Control Point, seizing 500 grams of suspected illegally imported live beetles with an estimated market value of about $1,900.
Customs officers yesterday intercepted a 68-year-old incoming local woman at the Arrival Hall of the control point. Upon examination, the batch of suspected illegally imported live beetles was seized from her trolley, and she was subsequently arrested. The case was handed over to the Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department for follow-up investigation.
Customs reminds members of the public that importing any plant pest or any plant infected by or infested with a plant pest into Hong Kong without prior authorisation issued by the Director of Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation is an offence.
Under the Plant (Importation and Pest Control) Ordinance, any person found guilty of illegally importing a plant pest without authorisation is liable to a maximum fine of $10,000 and imprisonment for six months.
Hong Kong Customs detects suspected case of illegal importing of live plant pests Source: HKSAR Government Press Releases

articles.title: IMD Issues Red Alert For Extremely Heavy Rainfall In Parts Of Rajasthan, Gujarat And Maharashtra - The Right News
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
articles.full_text:
IMD Issues Red Alert For Extremely Heavy Rainfall In Parts Of Rajasthan, Gujarat And Maharashtra
5 hours ago
0
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a red alert for very heavy to extremely heavy rainfall over East Rajasthan, Gujarat and Madhya Maharashtra today.
The weather agency has also forecast heavy rainfall over Himachal Pradesh, Konkan, Goa, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, Punjab, Saurashtra, Kutch and Uttarakhand during the next two days. Similar weather conditions are expected over the North Eastern region during the next two to three days.
The IMD said thunderstorms accompanied by lightning and gusty winds are likely over Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Puducherry, Karaikal, West Bengal, Jammu and Kashmir, Jharkhand, Kerala, Mahe and Telangana today.
According to the IMD, the maximum temperature in the national capital is expected to be between 34 and 36 degrees Celsius, while the minimum temperature is likely to remain between 24 and 26 degrees Celsius today.

articles.title: Monsoon weather Live updates: Gujarat on red alert, IMD advises extreme caution - India Today
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
articles.full_text:
Monsoon rain: No relief for Rajasthan, Gujarat; more rain to follow
IMD, in its latest update, has said that due to a low-pressure area over southeast Rajasthan, isolated exceptionally heavy rainfall is likely to continue over Gujarat state on July 24, 25 and isolated extremely heavy rainfall over Madhya Maharashtra on Friday.
Monsoon weather live: Western railways ops severely hit
Operation of trains by Western Railways has been severely hit as heavy rain floods tracks.
Monsoon LIVE: More rains to hit Himalayas
Fairly widespread to widespread rainfall is likely over Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, Ladakh, and Uttarakhand during 24th-30th July.
Monsoon rain: Assam death toll jumps to 41
The flood situation in Assam remains severe as the death toll climbed to 47 following six more fatalities reported within the last 24 hours. According to the latest bulletin issued by the Assam State Disaster Management Authority (ASDMA), over 7.21 lakh people across 11 districts are currently affected by the deluge.
Monsoon LIVE: Marble mines collapse in Rajasthan's Makrana after heavy rain
Nearly a dozen marble mines caved in at Makrana, the famed marble town in Rajasthan's Didwana-Kuchaman district, after heavy monsoon rains combined with extensive excavation caused the ground to give way.
Fortunately, no casualties were reported, as mining operations had already been suspended due to the rainy season.
According to local officials, years of deep excavation had left the mines vulnerable. As heavy rainfall poured into the excavated pits from all sides, the saturated ground became unstable, triggering a chain reaction in which one mine after another collapsed.
Weather today: Helicopter rescue operations begin in flood-hit Valsad
Flood Relief Operations Begin by Helicopter in Gujarat's Valsad; Man Rescued After Being Stranded on Electric Pole
- A helicopter rescue operation has been launched in flood-hit Valsad district of Gujarat.
- A man stranded on an electric pole in Salvav, Vapi, was rescued.
- He had climbed the pole yesterday to save his life and had been waiting for rescue ever since.
- The helicopter will now help deliver relief and carry out rescues in areas where boats are unable to reach.
Amid the worsening flood situation in Gujarat's Valsad district, authorities have intensified relief and rescue operations.
Weather live: Dhamni Dam Supplying Water to Vasai Virar Fills to 97 Percent
Palghar district has received a staggering 2,664 mm of rainfall since June 1, making it one of Maharashtra's wettest regions this monsoon. The relentless downpours have significantly boosted water levels in the Dhamni Dam, part of the Surya water supply project, with the reservoir now reaching 97% of its storage capacity. To prevent the dam from overflowing and maintain safe water levels, authorities have begun a large-scale release of water downstream.
Weather live: Isro captures massive Arabian Sea vortex
Fresh satellite images from ISRO's INSAT-3DR weather satellite have captured a spectacular view of a massive atmospheric vortex spinning over the Arabian Sea, revealing the weather engine responsible for the torrential monsoon rains that have inundated large parts of Gujarat. Read More
Weather today love: Satellite captures monsoon roaring
Monsoon rain is roaring across western India. Isro's INSAT-3DS has captured a stunning image as rain clouds shroud India.
Monsoon rain today: Ahmedabad drenched, Valsad flooded
Ahmedabad has been drenched in extreme rain as monsoon mayhem continues across Gujarat.
Monsoon Live: Southwest Monsoon remains active over west India
Satellite imagery from the India Meteorological Department (IMD) shows a dense cloud mass spread across western India, with intense convective clouds over Gujarat and parts of Maharashtra.
The system continues to fuel widespread heavy rainfall across the region, with the IMD warning that active monsoon conditions are likely to persist over the next few days.
Monsoon Updates Live: Delhi sees a cool, pleasant morning
Delhi woke up to cool and pleasant weather on Friday, July 24, with the IMD reporting below-normal maximum temperatures across the city.
Safdarjung recorded a maximum of 34.5Â°C, while Ayanagar was the coolest at 32.5Â°C.
Light rainfall was recorded at most weather stations, helping keep temperatures in check amid the active monsoon spell.
Monsoon Updates Live: IMD issues multiple alerts across Maharashtra
The India Meteorological Department has issued red alerts for Palghar and Pune, warning of extremely heavy rainfall on Friday.
Orange alerts are in place for Mumbai, Thane, Raigad, Ratnagiri, Sindhudurg, Nashik and several other districts, while parts of Marathwada and Vidarbha remain under yellow alerts as the active monsoon is expected to persist.
Monsoon Live: Watch Gujarat inundated after heavy downpours
Watch visuals from Gujarat as relentless heavy rain inundates roads, homes and neighbourhoods, leaving several areas submerged under flood-like conditions.
Monsoon Live Update: Gujarat's Valsad gets nearly Delhi's annual rainfall in just 24 hours
Gujarat's Valsad district witnessed one of its heaviest rain spells in recent years, with Umbergaon receiving 906 mm of rainfall between 6 am on July 23 and 6 am on July 24.
The extreme total is nearly equal to Delhi's average annual rainfall, highlighting the intensity of the ongoing monsoon surge that has triggered widespread flooding across the state.
Monsoon Live Update: Ahmedabad records historic levels of rain
Ahmedabad recorded its highest July rainfall of the 21st century during the 12-hour period between the night of July 23 and the morning of July 24, triggering widespread flooding, waterlogging and transport disruptions across the city.
Weather experts attributed the deluge to a stalled monsoon system fed by moisture from the Arabian Sea.
The IMD has warned that heavy rainfall is likely to continue across parts of Gujarat.

articles.title: China issues orange alert for Typhoon Noul, Level-IV emergency response - Xinhua
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
articles.full_text:
China issues orange alert for Typhoon Noul, Level-IV emergency response-Xinhua
China issues orange alert for Typhoon Noul, Level-IV emergency response
Source: Xinhua
Editor: huaxia
2026-07-24 14:42:15
BEIJING, July 24 (Xinhua) -- China's national observatory on Friday morning issued an orange alert, the second-highest in its four-tier system, for Typhoon Noul, the 12th typhoon of the year, as it forms and heads toward the coast of southeastern China.
At 5 a.m. on Friday, the center of Typhoon Noul was located about 560 km northeast of Manila, the Philippines, on the ocean surface. It is forecast to move west by north at a speed of 25-30 km per hour, according to the National Meteorological Center.
It is expected to make landfall along the coast from Zhuhai in south China's Guangdong to Zhangpu in Fujian in east China on Saturday night through Sunday morning, after which it will turn northward and gradually weaken in intensity.
From 8 a.m. on Friday to 8 a.m. on Saturday, heavy to torrential rainfall is expected in parts of eastern and southern Taiwan Island.
Meanwhile, strong winds are expected in the northeastern part of the South China Sea, the Bashi Channel, the sea areas east of Taiwan, the Taiwan Strait, the southern part of the East China Sea, as well as coastal areas of Taiwan, Fujian and the central and eastern parts of Guangdong.
The country's State Flood Control and Drought Relief Headquarters on Friday activated a Level-IV emergency response for flood and typhoon control in Guangdong and Fujian, according to the Ministry of Emergency Management.
In response to the approaching typhoon, Guangdong has activated a series of emergency measures.
Passenger ferry routes in affected areas have been suspended, while passenger vessels have halted operations. Thousands of offshore workers, including those on oil platforms, will finish evacuation as of noon on Friday, the provincial maritime safety administration said.
Rescue vessels, helicopters, tugboats and cleanup ships have been deployed for emergency response purposes.
China has a four-tier, color-coded weather warning system for typhoons, with red representing the most severe warning, followed by orange, yellow and blue. It also has a four-tier flood-control emergency response system, with Level I being the most severe. ■

articles.full_text:
A Heat Advisory is in place until Saturday at 9pm
Published: Jul. 23, 2026 at 5:33 PM MDT|Updated: 6 hours ago
Copyright 2026 KMVT. All rights reserved.
Skip to contentA Heat Advisory is in place until Saturday at 9pm
Chief Meteorologist Avaionia Smith: July 23, 2026— Happy Thursday! The heat is cranking up over the next several day! A Heat Advisory is in effect now until 9pm on Saturday. High temperatures are in the upper 90s today and triple digits expected on Friday. Remember to stay hydrated and practice heat safety tips, because heat related illnesses are possible if you are exposed to the dangerous heat for a long period of time. There is an isolated chance for storms on Friday afternoon, but clearing up by Friday night. We will remain dry through the rest of the weekend and into next week. Stay cool.
Published: Jul. 23, 2026 at 5:33 PM MDT|Updated: 6 hours ago
